The tornado began roughly 0.5 miles west of HWY 287 where several trees were damaged along with some outbuildings. The tornado then continued northeast where it damaged a home, an outbuilding was destroyed, and several pine trees were snapped at the trunk. Two by fours were impaled into the roof of the home as well. Roughly 30% of roof was peeled off of the home. The tornado then continued east towards HWY 287 where tree damage was found near Little Cypress Creek. This was the end of the Little Cypress Tornado.

A tornado touched down east of HWY 111, just south of C Davis Loop where it caused some minor tree damage. The tornado moved northeast where it impacted a structure, tearing away some of the roofing. Minor tree damage was also noted on the property. Several pine trees were snapped off on S Jeane Rd. Pine tree damage was noted on Fal Rd as the tornado crossed. Further tree damage could be seen north of Fal Rd, but is inaccessible. Further analysis may be needed to extend the track.

Photos received via social media showing an area of trees damaged. Several trees and power poles are snapped off 10 to 20 feet off of the ground. Possible tornado. Location is approximate and time estimated from radar.

Ft. Johnson Range Control reported trees down in the Mill Creek and Range 15 training areas. This damage is in association with a TDS noted on KPOE Radar. Access to the area is limited and further analysis will be completed using satellite imagery.
Reports of damage in the Boyce area. This may have been a tornado and will be surveyed tomorrow.

The Boyce Tornado began between HWY 49 and HWY 1 in Rapides Parish, where it downed several power lines. From there it continued northeast where it damaged homes as it crossed HWY 1. It then continued northeast into the town of Boyce, damaging several more homes, destroying outbuildings, and damaging various trees in the middle of Boyce. The tornado then continued further northeast, crossing the Red River into Shreveport’s CWA. Weak tree damage was found by WFO SHV on the north side of the Red River associated with this same tornado. The tornado damaged trees along Aaron Slayer Plantation Rd in Grant Parish, until it lifted around 11:57 AM.

A NWS damage survey revealed that 70 to 100 mph wind gusts damaged 13 large transmission lines across rural Acadia Parish. In addition to these lines, number trees, smaller power poles, and several homes and outbuildings were also heavily damaged. The width of the damage swath was approximately 3 miles which began just east of the Mermateau River, and extended to neighborhoods south of Eunice. Some of the greatest damage was large trees falling on homes.

Several photos received of many trees down in the community of Ritchie, LA southwest of Eunice. Several homes impacted, one with a tree through the middle of the structure. Numerous power outages and power lines down in the community as well.

A thunderstorm containing damaging descending rear inflow jet winds up to 90 mph created a swath of damage across northern Rapides Parish on May 26th 2025. Damage extended along a path roughly from the Rapides Sheriffs Office Shooting Range, through northern Pineville, and into rural areas of northeast Rapides Parish. Damage consisted of large trees falling into several homes, outbuildings ripped apart, and power poles pushed down.

An EF-0 tornado touched down near the intersection of Palermo Drive and Evangeline Street in Sulphur. The tornado then tracked across McMurry Park and into the Maplewood Neighborhood. Several homes in the area sustained minor damage, including damage to roofs, awnings, and fences. There was also tree damage, and a few power lines were pulled from their poles. The tornado continued over Lowe`s before crossing I-10 and dissipating near the intersection of Pete Manena Road and Bayou D`Inde Road. Tree damage was also observed along I-10 where the tornado crossed the interstate.
